topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox. Subject line: "Regarding your recent submission." My fingers hovered over the 'delete' button, a faint, almost imperceptible twitch in my jaw. I clicked it open anyway. The article, my meticulously researched piece on urban planning, was undeniably mine. The byline, however, bore a stranger's name. A name I didn't recognize, a name associated with a completely different publication. I scrolled through the text, the words feeling suddenly distant, like watching a movie through thick glass. I didn't feel a sharp pang of betrayal, just a slow, heavy settling in my chest. I closed the laptop lid with a soft, deliberate click, the sound echoing in the quiet apartment. There was nothing urgent to address, nothing that demanded a reaction. I stood up, stretched, and walked to the kitchen, the lukewarm coffee tasting exactly as it always did.

[story 2]

When the notification pinged, I glanced at it. Another piece of correspondence. I opened it, my gaze sweeping over the familiar formatting, the familiar arguments I had laid out on the page. Then, my eyes snagged on the attribution. A different author. A complete stranger had somehow claimed ownership of my work. A slight sigh escaped my lips, barely audible over the low hum of the refrigerator. I reread the opening paragraph, a detached assessment passing over the prose. It was just text. It had been published. The implications felt negligible, like a misplaced pebble on a vast beach. I set the screen down, turning away to look out the window at the indifferent rush of traffic below. I didn't feel the need to contact anyone, or even to argue. It was just a fact now, filed away in the quiet corners of my mind.

[story 3]

The news arrived via a shared drive link. I navigated to the file, my expression remaining placid. The headline screamed my topic, my analysis, my voice. But beneath it, the author credit was wrong. Someone else's signature adorned the piece. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning softly under my weight. A flicker of something—perhaps annoyance—crossed my features, but it faded almost instantly, leaving behind a blank neutrality. Why should this matter? The work was out there. It was being read. I picked up my phone, intending to check unrelated news, the whole situation already receding into the background noise of my day. There was no surge of anger, no sudden need to correct the error. Just a quiet acknowledgment of an administrative oversight, a minor inconvenience that required no significant emotional expenditure.
